|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-europe-44184985 |title=Cannes 2018: Japanese indie Shoplifters wins Palme d'Or |work=BBC News |date=19 May 2018 |access-date=20 May 2018 |archive-date=18 February [[Anna Karina]] from [[Jean-Luc Godard]]'s 1965 film ''[[Pierrot le Fou]]''. It is the second time the festival poster was inspired by Godard's film after his 1963 film ''[[Contempt (film)|Contempt]]'' at the [[2016 Cannes Film Festival|2016 festival]]. According to festival's official statement, the poster is inspired by and paid tribute to the work of French photographer Georges Pierre.[[File:Cannes 2018 11.jpg|thumb|Demonstration by female The issue prompted Juror Ava DuVernay, who made ''[[13th (film)|13th]]'' for Netflix, to make a plea for "flexibility of thought".{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/entertainment-arts-44046654|title=Blanchett hopes for Cannes equality|last=Smith|first=Neil|date=8 May 2018|work=BBC News|access-date=14 July 2018|language=en-GB|archive-date=11 May 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180511214735/http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/entertainment-arts-44046654|url-status=live}} In March and April 2018, weeks before general delegate [[Thierry Frémaux]] was set to unveil the official selection, reports suggested streaming service [[Netflix]] was to pull its already-selected films from premiering at the festival in retaliation for the barring of Netflix films from competing.{{cite web|url=https://www.vanityfair.com/hollywood/2018/04/netflix-threatens-to-pull-five-films-from-cannes-film-festival|title=Netflix Threatens to Pull Five Films from Cannes|publisher=Vanity Fair|access-date=6 April 2018|date=6 April 2018|language=en|archive-date=22 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181122033511/https://www.vanityfair.com/hollywood/2018/04/netflix-threatens-to-pull-five-films-from-cannes-film-festival|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.indiewire.com/2018/04/netflix-cannes-2018-jeremy-saulnier-orson-welles-1201950130/|title=Netflix Vs. Cannes: Filmmakers Respond to Battle That Turns Movies Into 'Collateral Damage'|publisher=Indiewire|access-date=8 April 2018|date=7 April 2018|language=en|archive-date=1 May 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180501182527/http://www.indiewire.com/2018/04/netflix-cannes-2018-jeremy-saulnier-orson-welles-1201950130/|url-status=live}} They were still allowed to premiere in other sections, and many reportedly opted for an Out of Competition berth. The films affected were [[Alfonso Cuarón]]'s ''[[Roma (2018 film)|Roma]]'', [[Morgan Neville]]'s ''[[They'll Love Me When I'm Dead]]'', [[Orson Welles]]' final film ''[[The Other Side of the Wind]]'', [[Paul Greengrass]]' ''[[22 July (film)|22 July]]'', and [[Jeremy Saulnier]]'s ''[[Hold the Dark]]''. Ultimately, Netflix pulled all of their films from selection.{{cite web|url=https://variety.com/2018/film/news/netflix-cannes-rule-change-ted-sarandos-interview-exclusive-1202750473/|title=Netflix Pulls Out of Cannes Following Rule Change (EXCLUSIVE)|publisher=Variety|access-date=12 April 2018|date=11 April 2018|language=en|archive-date=12 April 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180412030046/http://variety.com/2018/film/news/netflix-cannes-rule-change-ted-sarandos-interview-exclusive-1202750473/|url-status=live}} Notably, in the press conference announcement, Frémaux commented that he wanted ''[[The Other Side of the Wind]]'' and had planned to screen it as a special screening with the Welles-related documentary ''[[They'll Love Me When I'm Dead]]''.{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WuR18tZvDng|title=Festival de Cannes - Sélection Officielle du Festival de Cannes 2018|website=[[YouTube]]|access-date=12 April 2018|date=12 April 2018|archive-date=3 May 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180503092124/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WuR18tZvDng|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://theplaylist.net/cannes-netflix-debate-20180412/|title=Thierry Fremaux Says Netflix Is "Welcome In Cannes" In Response To Streaming Service's Latest Move|publisher=The Playlist|access-date=13 April 2018|date=12 April 2018|language=en|archive-date=30 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200930081349/https://theplaylist.net/cannes-netflix-debate-20180412/|url-status=live}} He also noted that he had selected ''[[Roma (2018 film)|Roma]]'' for competition. === Lars Von Trier === Danish film director [[Lars von Trier]] returned to Cannes with his film ''[[The House That Jack Built (2018 film)|The House That Jack Built]]'', after he was declared "''[[persona non grata]]''" at the [[2011 Cannes Film Festival|2011 festival]].{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/entertainment-arts-44104709|title=Cannes 2018: Festival awaits return of 'persona non grata' Lars von Trier|work=BBC News|date=14 May 2018|access-date=14 May 2018|archive-date=29 April 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200429044134/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/entertainment-arts-44104709|url-status=live}} === Gender equality === The chair of the jury [[Cate Blanchett]] has called for gender parity at the Cannes Film Festival, calling it "almost a gladiatorial sport". However, she concedes that there has been improvements and the change "won't happen overnight". During the festival, 82 female film professionals, led by Jury president [[Cate Blanchett]] and veteran director [[Agnès Varda]], took part to a demonstration on the red carpet, demanding more [[Gender equality|equality between men and women]] in the [[film industry]], notably the end of the [[Gender pay gap|pay gap]].[https://www.vox.com/culture/2018/5/13/17347738/cannes-womens-protest-march-film-festival-cate-blanchett-agnes-varda-timesup-red-carpet 82 women protested gender inequity in the film industry on the red carpet at Cannes] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180528215712/https://www.vox.com/culture/2018/5/13/17347738/cannes-womens-protest-march-film-festival-cate-blanchett-agnes-varda-timesup-red-carpet |date=28 May 2018 }}, ''[[Vox (website)|Vox]]'', 13 May 2018 The festival launched a sexual harassment hotline in partnership with France’s Ministry of Gender Equality where victims of harassment and abuse could receive support and guidance by calling a dedicated number.{{cite news |title=Cannes Film Festival launches hotline to support victims of sexual harassment and abuse |url=https://filmindustry.network/cannes-film-festival-launches-hotline-to-support-victims-of-sexual-harassment-and-abuse/32903 |publisher=Film Industry Network |date=9 May 2018}}{{cite news |title='Victims know they can call us' – Cannes sexual harassment hotline up and running |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2018/may/11/cannes-film-festival-sexual-harassment-phone-hotline-victims |work=The Guardian |date=11 May 2018 |access-date=29 June 2019 |archive-date=29 June 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190629233312/https://www.theguardian.com/film/2018/may/11/cannes-film-festival-sexual-harassment-phone-hotline-victims |url-status=live }} === Homophobia === French actor [[Éric Bernard (actor)|Éric Bernard]], who was attending as part of the cast of the LGBTQ+ movie ''[[Sauvage (film)|Sauvage]]'', blamed the French film industry for the decline in his career, alleging that it was a consequence of homophobic bias. During an interview with ''[[20 minutes (France)|20 minutes]]'' in 2023, Bernard recalled that he went to the Cannes festival in 2018 and walked hand in hand with his husband, which caused Bernard's manager to scold him, advising to hide his relationship to protect his career.{{cite news |last1=Beaudonnet |first1=Laure |title=Homophobie dans le cinéma : Farid-Eric Bernard, la dégringolade après le film « Sauvage » à cause de son coming-out ? |url=https://www.20minutes.fr/arts-stars/cinema/4055858-20231005-homophobie-cinema-farid-eric-bernard-degringolade-apres-film-sauvage-cause-coming-out
